Our proposal replaces the fixed weights of a fully-connected layer with a function of an additional input (reliability score) at each input, mimicking the ability of cortex to up- and down-weight inputs based on the presence of other data.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended contribution as an application of conditional weight generation to missingness/reliability signals, and the biological framing is a reasonable motivation. However, replacing a layer’s weights by a learned function of another input is closely related to established ideas such as hypernetworks, dynamic filter networks, conditional computation, gating, and FiLM-style modulation. Looking ahead in the provided context, the related-work discussion compares mainly against imputation, Bayesian deep learning, and attention, but does not appear to situate the proposed MFCL against these more directly related conditional-parameterization methods. The method may still be novel in its restricted use for feature reliability/missingness in tabular settings, but the broad phrasing “a novel neural network modification” overstates what has been established without a more precise novelty claim.

comments.title: Robustness claim is broader than the mixed comparative results support
comments.quote: Models with modulating layers were more robust against degradation of data quality by introducing additional missingness at evaluation time.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a high-level summary, and the later experiments do test degradation under random and non-random missingness, which is appropriate. But checking the results described in the full context, the advantage is not uniform: for random missingness, FC+Mod and sometimes VAEAC are reported as best, with MFCL/MFCL+ only close; for non-random missingness, MFCL is stronger on some datasets/metrics but not consistently dominant; and the text itself notes mixed behavior across AUROC and AUPRC. Thus the abstract’s unqualified statement that models with modulating layers “were more robust” could lead readers to infer a consistent robustness improvement across tasks, metrics, and baselines. A more defensible statement would specify that robustness gains were most evident under some non-random missingness conditions and that performance was often comparable rather than uniformly better.

comments.title: Claim about absence of tabular masking results appears under-supported
comments.quote: No similar results with masking have been shown in tabular data, to our knowledge.
comments.explanation: I see why the authors contrast image masking augmentation with tabular missingness: obscuring pixels in images and masking whole tabular features are not identical problems. The phrase “to our knowledge” also softens the claim. Still, the statement is quite broad. There is substantial prior work on feature dropout, denoising autoencoders, robust training with corrupted tabular features, and missingness augmentation for tabular models. Even if none exactly match the cited computer-vision masking result, a careful reader could reasonably expect this sentence to acknowledge or distinguish those lines of work. As written, it risks overstating the gap in the literature unless the authors narrow the claim to a specific setting or provide a more complete comparison.

comments.title: “Reliability” terminology is potentially inverted relative to later use as noise standard deviation
comments.quote: Those weights could be modulated based on a variety of inputs, but we focus on input reliability as a modulating signal.
comments.explanation: The intended idea is clear: the modulating signal conveys how much trust the network should place in each input. However, elsewhere in the provided context the reliability experiment defines the modulating signal as the standard deviation of artificially added noise, where larger values mean lower reliability. Missingness flags likewise indicate unreliability rather than reliability. A learned MLP can of course learn either monotonic direction, so this is not a mathematical failure of the method. The remaining issue is semantic: calling the signal “reliability” may mislead readers into assuming higher values denote more reliable measurements, when the implementation appears closer to an uncertainty/noise/missingness indicator. The paper should clarify the convention explicitly, e.g. “reliability/uncertainty signal” or “noise standard deviation used as an inverse reliability score.”

comments.title: Reduced-overfitting claim relies on an unspecified restriction of the modulation structure
comments.quote: A restricted structure of modulating inputs and effects on the modulated layer reduces the likelihood of severe over-fitting and complexity of the estimation problem.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intent: conditioning only a selected layer on reliability signals could be less flexible than allowing arbitrary network-wide conditioning, and therefore might regularize the model. But the claim depends on what the “restricted structure” actually is. In the later methods, the main formal definition is essentially \(W_{mod}=g(m)\), with \(g\) an MLP, which could generate a full weight matrix and may introduce many additional parameters. The provided context does not spell out what constraints are imposed on the modulation inputs, the output dimensionality, sharing pattern, rank, sparsity, or other restrictions that would justify the claimed reduction in overfitting or estimation complexity. This may be resolved in an appendix, but as stated in the main narrative it is an unsupported methodological assertion rather than a demonstrated consequence of the architecture.

comments.title: Description of chained equations as Gibbs sampling is only conditionally accurate
comments.quote: Single or multiple imputation using chained equations (Gibbs sampling of missing data) is popular due to its relative accuracy and ability to account for imputation uncertainty (Azur et al., 2011).
comments.explanation: The authors are correctly pointing to MICE/chained equations as a standard imputation family, and Bayesian implementations can resemble a Gibbs sampler when drawing from compatible full conditional distributions. The parenthetical “Gibbs sampling of missing data,” however, is too categorical. Fully conditional specification by chained equations is not necessarily a coherent joint Bayesian model, and common implementations may use iterative regression-style conditional models rather than exact Gibbs sampling from a joint posterior. This is a localized technical imprecision, but it matters because the claimed ability to account for imputation uncertainty depends on the specific multiple-imputation procedure and assumptions, not merely on using chained equations.

comments.title: Missing input values still require an explicit numeric representation
comments.quote:
_hout_ = _f_ ( **Wmod** _· hin_ + _b_ ) _,_ (1)

These experiments used modulating signals of missing value flags and input reliability values of noisy data. We can think of missing values as a special case included in reliability where _missing_ implies completely unreliable measurement.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended distinction: the missingness flag or reliability score modulates the weights, so the model can learn how to use the pattern of missingness rather than relying only on an imputation preprocessor. However, the layer formula still multiplies **Wmod** by _hin_. If entries of _hin_ are actually missing/NaN, this operation is undefined. Therefore the method must still choose some numeric placeholder for missing feature values, such as zero after normalization, the mean, a sentinel value, or a learned mask-aware encoding. I checked the surrounding context: the baselines explicitly mention mean imputation or other imputation algorithms, but the MFCL/MFCL+ preprocessing for the actual missing entries is not specified here. This matters because the chosen placeholder can affect both performance and fairness of comparison to FC+Mod. The authors may mean that they avoid a separate statistical imputation model, but the paper should state exactly how missing _hin_ values are encoded before the modulated layer.

comments.title: The modulation function output and structural restrictions are underspecified
comments.quote:
**Wmod** = _g_ ( _m_ ) _,_ (2)

where _m_ is the modulating signal input and _g_ is the function that is defined by a multilayer perceptron.
comments.explanation: The high-level idea is clear: an MLP maps reliability or missingness indicators to the weights used by the first fully connected layer. What is not clear is the exact mathematical object produced by _g_. If **Wmod** is a full dense matrix for each individual sample, then _g_ must output `n_out × n_in` values per example, which can be parameter-heavy and computationally nonstandard. Earlier prose mentions that a restricted structure of modulating inputs and effects reduces overfitting and estimation complexity, so I looked for that restriction here, but the equation presents a completely general generated weight matrix. The appendix may list architectures, but the central method definition does not specify whether the modulation is full-matrix, diagonal, row-wise, column-wise, low-rank, elementwise gating, or otherwise constrained. This affects reproducibility and the interpretation of the claimed robustness, because these choices substantially change parameter count and inductive bias.

comments.title: MFCL+ residual weight term **W0** is not clearly defined as fixed, pretrained, or trainable
comments.quote:
Another variant of the MFCL (MFCL+) adds a skip connection to the transfer step and modifies the weights ( **W0** ) of a starting network,

**Wmod** + = _g_ ( _m_ ) + **W0** _._ (3)
comments.explanation: The intended formula is presumably a residual version such as **W**\_{mod}^{+} = _g_(_m_) + **W0**, and the apparent placement of the plus sign may be an OCR artifact rather than an author error. The substantive ambiguity remains in the definition of **W0**. A careful reader cannot tell whether **W0** is randomly initialized and trained jointly with _g_, copied from a pretrained ordinary fully connected network and then frozen, copied and fine-tuned, or simply a standard learned base weight matrix. These alternatives lead to different optimization procedures and different comparisons against the FC baseline. The surrounding sentence says “starting network,” which even suggests possible pretraining, but no such procedure is described in this passage. The authors should explicitly define the training status and initialization of **W0**.

comments.title: Noise standard deviation is called a reliability score although it is an uncertainty/noise magnitude
comments.quote: Reliability was quantified by the standard deviation of the noise that was artificially added to the signal.
comments.explanation: I see what the authors are trying to do: provide the modulation network with a per-feature signal that tells it how trustworthy a noisy observation is. But standard deviation of additive noise is conventionally an uncertainty or error scale, not a reliability score; larger values mean lower reliability. This is not necessarily mathematically wrong, because the MLP can learn that larger values should be down-weighted. Still, it conflicts with the surrounding terminology in which reliability represents quality/trustworthiness and missingness is “completely unreliable.” Without clarifying whether the input is reliability, unreliability, variance, standard deviation, or an inverse transformation such as precision, a reader could reasonably misinterpret the monotonic direction of the modulation signal or reproduce the experiment with the reciprocal quantity.

comments.title: Related-work claims about Bayesian deep learning and attention are somewhat overgeneralized
comments.quote: Incorporating uncertainty measurements into deep neural networks has also been approached with Bayesian deep learning methods, (Wang and Yeung, 2016; Wilson, 2020) which has a complex, assumption laden structure using probabilistic graphical models. One simpler variation of Bayesian deep learning is the Gaussian process deep neural network which assigns an uncertainty level at the output based on the missing data so that inputs with greater missingness lead to higher uncertainty (Bradshaw et al., 2017). Our method makes use of meaningful missingness patterns as opposed to treating it as a problem that leads to lower confidence in outputs. Our approach is superficially similar to attention mechanisms: the lower quality inputs receive less importance, but attention networks employ a complex feedback mechanism to assign the attention distribution using the input sequence and the query and is thus difficult to scale for long time-varying inputs (Kim et al., 2017).
comments.explanation: The authors are reasonably positioning MFCL against uncertainty-aware and attention-like approaches, and this is a related-work paragraph rather than a formal theorem. Still, several statements are broader than standard usage supports. Bayesian neural networks do not all require explicit probabilistic graphical-model machinery in the same sense, and Gaussian-process/deep-kernel methods generally model predictive uncertainty rather than specifically assigning uncertainty “based on the missing data” unless missingness is explicitly modeled. Similarly, vanilla sequence attention has scaling issues, but attention mechanisms are a broad family, including feature-wise attention and efficient variants; they do not all require the same query-sequence setup or have the same scaling limitations. This does not undermine the proposed method, but it risks overstating the contrast with prior work.

comments.title: Imputation inputs with natural and artificial missingness are not fully specified
comments.quote: The baseline autoencoder for imputation was trained by adding artificial missingess to the input values at random at a ratio of 25%. The loss function at the output layer calculated the mean squared error between the output values and the original values of the artificially removed values. The naturally missing data was included in the training dataset but not included in the loss function due to the absence of a known value to compare to.
comments.explanation: I read this as a denoising-autoencoder-style imputation setup, which is a standard and reasonable approach: mask some observed values, reconstruct them, and exclude truly unobserved entries from the reconstruction loss. What remains unclear after checking the surrounding context is how the inputs containing both naturally missing and artificially removed values are actually represented to the neural network. A standard autoencoder or MLP cannot consume NaNs directly, so reproducibility depends on whether missing entries were filled with zero, mean values, learned mask tokens, normalized constants, or something else, and whether the same placeholder was used for natural and artificial missingness. It is also unclear whether the 25% artificial missingness was sampled only from originally observed entries, which would normally be required because the original value is needed for the loss. The paper discusses missingness flags as modulating signals elsewhere, but for the baseline FC autoencoder and the exact MFCL/MFCL+ inputs this preprocessing choice is still a key methodological detail.

comments.title: Non-random and variable missingness protocols for imputation are underspecified
comments.quote: The models were tested using variable artificial missingness.
comments.explanation: The intent seems to be to evaluate imputation robustness under more than one missingness mechanism, and the later results distinguish random from non-random removal. However, this sentence does not specify the levels of missingness, how many repetitions or masks were used, or how the non-random missingness was generated for the imputation experiments. In classification and regression, non-random missingness is sometimes described as removing values above quantiles or removing complete features, but the imputation section does not say whether the same rule was used, whether the threshold was computed per feature on the training split only, or whether the same masked entries were used across models. Because imputation MSE can vary substantially depending on which variables and value ranges are masked, these missing details make the imputation comparison difficult to reproduce and interpret.

comments.title: Complete-feature removal experiment does not specify which features were removed
comments.quote: Figure 4 shows another example of non-random missingness by removing complete features.
comments.explanation: The motivation is clear: in deployment, an entire measurement may be unavailable because a site does not collect it. But the experimental protocol is not sufficiently specified. Removing one to five complete features can produce very different results depending on whether the removed features are chosen randomly, by missingness rate, by clinical availability, by predictive importance, or in a fixed prespecified order. I checked whether the surrounding text resolves this through the figure or the earlier methods, but it only reports the number of features removed. Since the paper draws robustness conclusions from this experiment, the feature-selection rule and any averaging over feature subsets should be stated.

comments.title: Order of train/test split and artificial training missingness is ambiguous for regression
comments.quote: For the regressor with missing flags as modulating signal, we introduced artificial non-random missingness into the dataset for training the regressor by removing the highest quartile of each variable.
comments.explanation: The described design is plausible: train the regression models under a controlled MNAR-like missingness pattern and then test robustness under additional missingness. However, the phrase “into the dataset for training” leaves an important ordering ambiguous. Later the paper says an 80:20 train/test split was used for each dataset, but it is not clear whether the highest-quartile thresholds were computed only on the training split or on the full dataset before splitting. If the latter, the masking rule uses information from the test distribution; even if labels are not used, this can still affect a benchmark protocol. It is also unclear whether the highest-quartile removal was applied only to the training split or to the full dataset before evaluation missingness was added. This is fixable, but the exact ordering matters for reproducibility and for avoiding inadvertent test-set leakage.

comments.title: Significance testing treats bootstrap replicates as repeated measurements
comments.quote: To test for statistical significance, we calculated repeated measure ANOVA on the bootstrapping results followed by paired t-test between different model pairs with correction for false discovery rate using Benjamini/Yekutieli method.
comments.explanation: Using paired bootstrapping to estimate confidence intervals is common and appropriate for paired model comparisons. The concern is the next step: bootstrap replicates are resampled pseudo-datasets, not independent experimental repeats. Treating the 1000 bootstrap draws as observations in a repeated-measures ANOVA and paired t-tests can yield anticonservative p-values because the effective sample size is not 1000 independent test sets. A permutation test, DeLong-style test for AUROC where applicable, a bootstrap test based on the distribution of paired differences, or reporting bootstrap confidence intervals for differences would be more standard. The multiple-comparison correction is a good instinct, but it does not by itself address the dependence/pseudoreplication issue.

comments.title: FC+Mod input description conflates missing values, missingness flags, and reliability
comments.quote: The second one applied constant value imputation (mean value) in addition to concatenating the missing value (input reliability) at the input layer (FC+Mod).
comments.explanation: From earlier context, I infer that FC+Mod means mean imputation plus concatenated missingness indicators, which is a sensible and common baseline. The wording here, however, says “concatenating the missing value (input reliability),” which could be read literally as concatenating the imputed/missing value itself rather than a binary missing-value flag or reliability score. Missingness flags and continuous reliability scores are conceptually related in the paper, but they are not identical experimental inputs. This is probably just imprecise wording, not a substantive method error, yet a careful reader trying to reproduce the regression baseline could be unsure whether the concatenated variable is a binary mask, a reliability score, or the imputed value.

comments.title: Inferring usefulness of missingness patterns from incomparable imputation loss scales
comments.quote: We also note that the range of losses in this dataset is very different between random and non-random removal which indicates the usefulness of meaningful missingness patterns in predicting the latent representation of the data.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended point: non-random missingness can be informative, and the authors want to argue that MFCL/MFCL+ exploit this structure better than a standard autoencoder. However, the specific evidence cited here does not by itself support that conclusion. Random and non-random removal evaluate reconstruction error on different subsets of values; if non-random removal removes high-quantile values, then the held-out targets can have a different scale, variance, and distance from the mean than randomly removed values. A different MSE range may therefore reflect the distribution of the removed values rather than the model's ability to use meaningful missingness patterns or learn a better latent representation. This concern is not resolved by the surrounding context, because the paper does not state that losses were normalized to make the random and non-random removal conditions directly comparable or that latent representations were separately analyzed.

comments.title: Claim that all networks learned the latent representation is not directly established
comments.quote: It appears that all the networks were able to learn that represnetation but with our architectures learning is better.
comments.explanation: The authors are reasonably trying to interpret lower imputation loss as evidence that the autoencoders captured useful structure in ACTFAST. Still, this statement goes beyond what the reported metric directly shows. Mean squared reconstruction error on artificially removed entries can demonstrate better imputation performance, but it does not establish that all networks learned the same latent representation, nor that the MFCL/MFCL+ learned a better representation rather than, for example, using the missingness flags more effectively at the input-output mapping level. This could be fixed by rephrasing as an interpretation of the imputation results, or by adding representation-level evidence. As written, the claim is stronger than the experiment supports.

comments.title: Overfitting explanation for null results on smaller datasets is speculative
comments.quote: For other datasets, we found no significant differences which could possibly due to the small size of datasets increasing the overfitting possibility in comparison with the ACTFAST dataset which is orders of magnitude larger.
comments.explanation: The proposed explanation is plausible: MFCL/MFCL+ add parameters, and smaller datasets such as Breast Cancer and Boston are more susceptible to variance and overfitting. I checked the surrounding methods and discussion, and the authors do acknowledge the large parameter count of the modulation network elsewhere. However, this passage does not report evidence of overfitting, such as training-versus-validation loss gaps, sensitivity to regularization, or variance across train/test splits. The absence of significant differences could also be due to weaker missingness signal, different feature correlations, lower task difficulty, or insufficient power. The wording includes 'could possibly,' which softens the claim, so this is not a major flaw, but it remains an under-supported causal explanation.

comments.title: Regression comparison mentions VAEAC despite regression baselines not defining it
comments.quote: In this task, while it appears that MFCL outperformed all other networks significantly in all conditions with VAEAC coming as a close second.
comments.explanation: The result being summarized is clear in intent: MFCL is reported as the best regression model, with another method close behind. The concern is that the methods section for the Boston regression task describes only two baseline regression networks: chained-regression/Iterative Imputer preprocessing and FC+Mod with mean imputation plus missing indicators. VAEAC is described earlier as a classification/imputation baseline, not as a regression baseline in Section 3.6. If VAEAC was in fact adapted and evaluated for regression, the regression methods are underspecified; if it was not, this sentence is inconsistent with the stated experimental design. This affects the interpretability of the regression comparison because readers cannot tell which models were actually included or how VAEAC was configured for a supervised regression task.

comments.title: Improved regression performance with more removed data may be confounded by informative missingness indicators
comments.quote: Interestingly, the performance either remained the same or improved with more data removed. This could possibly be due to uncorrelated inputs that have very little shared information especially given the high absolute values of loss.
comments.explanation: The observation is important and worth discussing, because performance improving as more inputs are removed is counterintuitive under ordinary missing-at-random degradation. I considered the experimental context: for Boston regression, non-random missingness was introduced by removing values above feature-wise quantiles, and missing flags were supplied to modulation/FC+Mod-style models. Under this design, the missingness indicator is not merely an availability flag; it encodes threshold information about the original feature value, e.g. whether the value exceeded a quantile. As the removal threshold changes, these binary indicators can themselves become predictive covariates. Thus, improved performance with more removal could reflect information leakage from the artificial MNAR mechanism rather than robustness to less information or uncorrelated inputs. The proposed explanation about uncorrelated inputs is possible but not justified by correlation analysis, ablations without flags, or comparison to a mechanism where missingness is informative but not directly threshold-encoding.

comments.title: Benefit over concatenating missingness indicators is overstated given MFCL parameter cost
comments.quote: This is a main benefit of the modulation strategy compared to the conventional strategy of concatenating a missing data indicator to the inputs, which doubles the input space and complicates the search for appropriate architectures and regularization strategies.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly note that concatenating one missingness indicator per feature doubles the raw input dimension, and this can affect architecture and regularization choices. However, the surrounding discussion also acknowledges that the MFCL modulation network has a large number of parameters, especially in its final layer, scaling with the product of the input and output sizes of the modulated fully connected layer. Given that, it is not clear that MFCL simplifies the architecture/regularization search relative to concatenation; it may shift or increase that burden. This is especially relevant because the methods state that modulation-network hyperparameters were selected by grid search. The claim would be more defensible if framed as a potential architectural separation benefit rather than a general 'main benefit' over concatenation.

comments.title: Plug-and-play claim is stronger than the training setup supports
comments.quote: By separating the two architectures, we can learn a plug-and-play modification for any classification task on the same inputs.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended advantage: the modulation component is separated from the main predictor and depends on the same reliability/missingness inputs, which could make it reusable in spirit. But in the architecture described earlier, the modulation network is jointly learned with the main task and outputs weights whose shape depends on the particular modulated layer dimensions. Its learned mapping is therefore tied not only to the input features but also to the downstream model architecture and task loss. It is not obvious that a learned modulation network can be plugged into 'any classification task on the same inputs' without retraining or matching hidden dimensions. This is a mild overclaim rather than a mathematical error, but it could mislead readers about reusability.
